segments.s573-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. شب (night) is the agent that performs the action; دامن (literally 'skirt, hem') is the image used for the night’s covering of روز (the day). اندر + کشید is a past action 'drew over' (کشید is past), so the clause literally reads 'when night drew the skirt over the day' with poetic inversion of elements for rhythm.
segments.s573-g2: درفش خور is a nominal phrase 'the banner (درفش) of the sun (خور)' — the genitive relation is shown by simple juxtaposition. آمد is the past tense 'came'; ز بالا پدید 'from on high appeared' places the source ('from above') before the stative/appearance verb پدید, a common poetic order.
segments.s573-g3: بفرمود شاپور is a polite/honorific past 'Shapur said/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'), and شد دبیر ('a scribe was made/appointed') uses شد as the past/resultive 'became/was made' — together meaning 'Shapur ordered so that a scribe be appointed.'
segments.s573-g4: قلم خواست is a past-tense verbal clause '(he) requested/asked for a pen' (خواست). The coordinating و links a series of objects (انقاس و مشک و حریر) so the single verb خواست applies to all following nouns; this is a conventional elliptical list of requested items.
segments.s573-g5: نوشتند is the past plural 'they wrote'; نامه is the direct object 'letters'. The prepositional phrase به هر مهتری is the indirect object 'to each/ every chief' (مهتری) introduced by به.
segments.s573-g6: This continues the list of recipients: به هر پادشاهی و هر کشوری — 'to every kingdom/king and every country/realm.' به marks the indirect object; the repeated هر emphasizes 'every' before each noun, and و simply links the two recipients.
segments.s573-g7: سرنامه کرد is 'he made/arranged a heading/title' (سرنامه = heading, کرد = made). آفرین مهان is the wording of that heading: an exclamatory/acclamatory phrase ('Afarin' = 'praise/hallelujah' or 'well done') followed by مهان (vocative 'guests' or 'visitors'). The sequence shows a verb 'made' followed by the title phrase as its object.
segments.s573-g8: ز ما بنده بر کردگار جهان is a humble formula: ز ما 'from us' or 'as for us', بنده 'servant' (self-effacing), بر + کردگار جهان 'toward/to the Creator of the world' where کردگار جهان is a genitive compound 'Creator of the world'. Word order here is poetic and compact but means roughly 'we are servants before the Creator of the world.'
segments.s573-g105: مهاری (noun) is the direct object 'a bridle' placed first for emphasis; به بینی او is a prepositional/locative phrase 'onto his nose' (به + noun) with او as the third-person pronoun 'him'; برنهاد is an archaic past tense verb 'placed on / put on' (3rd singular). The sequence Object + prepositional phrase + verb is poetic but means 'he put a bridle onto his nose.'
segments.s573-g106: چو is a comparative/concessive 'as' (introducing likeness). شاپور is the proper name/agent performing the action. زان is an archaic contraction 'from that / from it' (زان = از + آن). چرم خر is a noun phrase 'donkey-hide leather' (چرم 'leather' qualified by خر 'donkey'); کرد is the simple past 'made' or 'did'; یاد here is a concrete 'reminder/memento' (an object made to recall something). Overall: 'as Shapur, from that donkey-hide leather made a memento.'
segments.s573-g107: دو is the numeral 'two' modifying بند گران 'heavy fetters' (بند here = 'fetter, bond', گران = 'heavy/weighty'). برنهادش is the past verb 'placed on' with the enclitic -ش 'him' (object). به پای is the locative 'on/at the foot(s)' (i.e. 'on his feet'). Word order places the quantified noun phrase before the verb for emphasis: 'he placed two heavy fetters on his feet.'
segments.s573-g108: ببردش is a poetic/archaic verbal form meaning 'took him' / 'carried him' (here transitive with the enclitic -ش 'him'). همان is the demonstrative 'that same' specifying روزبان (a proper name or title). باز is the adverb 'back/again' and جای is 'place.' The clause order (verb + subject + adverb + place) is idiomatic in epic poetry: 'that same Rozban took him back to the place.'
segments.s573-g25: برآمد is the past verb ‘arose/appeared’ formed with the prefix بر + آمد; the clause reads subject + verb + source phrase: هیونی (plural/collective ‘monstrous creatures’) is the subject, بر آمد is the simple past ‘rose up’. ز هر سو دمان is a prepositional source phrase (‘from every side of the damaan/skirts’), with ز (poetic ‘from’) before the noun phrase (هر سو = ‘every side/direction’, دمان = ‘skirt/hem, edge’). Word order is Subject – Verb – Source (typical in older Persian poetry).
segments.s573-g26: This noun phrase functions adverbially/incidentally: ابا is here a poetic/orthographic form representing با (‘with’); نامه is the direct noun ‘letter’; شاه is the title ‘the king’; روشن‌روان is an epithet attached to the king (‘bright‑souled’). The whole chunk means ‘with the king’s letter, the bright‑souled’ and links to the previous clause as accompanying circumstance. (Note: ابا appears with a prosthetic alef in the manuscript form — grammatically it behaves like the preposition با.)
segments.s573-g27: ز + place noun gives the source/starting point: ز لشکرگه ‘from the army‑camp’. آمد is the simple past ‘he came’. سوی طیسفون is the goal phrase ‘toward Ctesiphon’ (سوی = toward(s), followed by the place name). The clause therefore states movement: ‘he came from the army‑camp toward Ctesiphon.’
segments.s573-g28: بی‌آزار is an adjective/adverb meaning ‘harmless/without harm’ and here qualifies the subject state. بنشست is the simple past ‘he sat (down)’ (بـ- + نشست is a common classical/perfective form). با رهنمون is a prepositional phrase ‘with a guide’ (با = with, رهنمون = guide/guidance) showing accompaniment. Word order: adverbial adjective – verb – accompaniment.
segments.s573-g29: چو here is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. تاج نیاکانش is the noun phrase ‘the crown of his ancestors’ (تاج = crown, نیاکانش = his ancestors). بر سر نهاد is a fixed verbal phrase: بر سر + نهاد (نهاد = placed, past of نهادن) meaning ‘placed upon his head’. The clause structure is temporal subordinate (چو …) + main clause that follows.
segments.s573-g30: ز دادار is a prepositional phrase ‘from/of the Creator’ (ز = from/of, دادار = the Creator). نیکی دهش is a compound meaning ‘gift/grant of goodness’ (نیکی = goodness, دهش = grant/gift). کرد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb ‘he did/made’ and here governs یاد as its object/complement: کرد یاد = ‘made remembrance/remembered’. The whole clause means ‘he remembered/made mention of the Creator’s gift of goodness.’
segments.s573-g31: بفرمود is an honorific/formal past ‘he commanded/ordered’. تا here introduces the result/commandive subordinate (‘so that’ / ‘that’), and شد is the past/passive auxiliary ‘became/was made’. The phrase به زندان دبیر places the patient/recipient before the location: دبیر (the scribe/secretary) + به زندان (‘to prison’) — together giving the result of the order: ‘he ordered that the scribe be put into prison.’ The syntax is order → subordinate clause introduced by تا + passive/result (شد).
segments.s573-g32: به introduces the indirect object or location ‘to/at’. انقاس here is likely a place or register name (manuscript reading uncertain), so the phrase means ‘at/in Anqās’. بنوشت is an archaic past form of نوشتن ‘he wrote’ (in classical poetry b- prefix often appears), followed by نام اسیر ‘the captive’s name’ (نام = name, اسیر = captive). The clause therefore says ‘he wrote the captive’s name at/in Anqās.’
segments.s573-g33: This segment gives a compounded numerical expression: هزار (thousand) + و (and) + صد (hundred) + و (and) + ده (ten) = 1,110. برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/amounted' and شمار ('count') is the noun it governs; word order here places the numerals before the verbal phrase 'برآمد شمار' = 'the count amounted to'.
segments.s573-g34: بزرگان روم = 'the great ones/leading men of Rome' (possessive relation: بزرگان + روم). آنک is a relative particle 'who/which' introducing the following qualification. بد + نامدار is a compound adjective meaning literally 'bad-famous' = 'ill‑famed' or 'of ill repute'; بد modifies نامدار to give the negative sense.
segments.s573-g35: همه خویش = 'all his own' (خویش is the possessive/kinship word here, written as خوی + ـش 'his'). و پیوند قیصر = 'and the bond/relations of the Caesar' (پیوند used for connected relatives or ties). بدند is an archaic 3rd‑person plural past copula 'they were' linking to the locative clause that follows.
segments.s573-g36: به روم اندرون = 'in(to) Rome's inner quarters' with به + مکان (here 'into/at'). ویژه is 'special/particular' or 'respectively', and مهتر is 'a chief/distinguished elder'; the final بدند repeats the 3rd‑person plural 'they were', so the clause reads 'they were inside Rome, each (or specially) a chief/elder.'
segments.s573-g37: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' or 'ruler'. ببریدشان is composed of the past stem ببرید (he cut) plus the object enclitic ـشان 'them' = 'he cut them'; the direct objects دست و پای (hands and feet) follow, literal listing of body parts. The verb is simple past with attached object pronoun.
segments.s573-g38: هرانکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who...'. بد بر بدی is a compact, poetic phrase: بد (bad) + بر (toward/onto) + بدی (evil) — 'who directs/turns bad toward evil' or more naturally 'who as an evil influence/guide leads to evil'. رهنمای is a noun 'guide/adviser' describing the person; the whole clause characterizes the punished persons as evil guides.
segments.s573-g39: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said' used here to introduce an indirect command. تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause ('so that' / 'that') and governs the following subjunctive. قیصر روم را is the definite object 'the Caesar of Rome' marked by the object particle را.
segments.s573-g40: بیارند is the present/subjunctive plural 'they may bring' (here used under تا after a command: 'that they bring'); in the supplied lemma/senses list there is no matching 'bring' sense, so the sense index is unknown. سالار آن بوم را is an appositive or further identification: 'the commander/ruler (سالار) of that land (آن بوم)' with را marking it as the object being brought. Grammatically, the subordinate verb (بیارند) takes قیصر روم را / سالار آن بوم را as its object(s), identifying who is to be brought.
segments.s573-g73: This is the protasis of a conditional clause introduced by اگر (if). یابم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative of یابیدن (literally “may I find/obtain” or “if I obtain”), here used with the sense of a hypothetical 'if I get/receive'. از تو marks the source ('from you'). به جان is an oath-like circumstantial phrase 'by my life' (به + جان), and زینهار is an interjection/particle ('zinehâr' = 'beware' / 'I implore you') appended as an emphatic admonition; the whole segment therefore reads ‘If I obtain from you — by my life, I beg you (zinehâr)…’
segments.s573-g74: This is the apodosis/result clause. به چشمم literally 'to my eye' (به + چشم + possessive suffix -م = 'my/—me') functions idiomatically as 'in my sight' or 'to my eyes'. شود is the present/future passive/impersonal auxiliary 'become / will be', and is followed by the nominative list 'گنج و دینار' (treasure and dinars). خوار is a predicate/adjective meaning 'despised/treated as worthless' that attaches to the coordinated nouns. Note that the -م on چشم is the possessive suffix 'my' (here separated in the word-list as م).
segments.s573-g75: یکی here has the sense of 'one / a single / mere' (a minimalist particle introducing a modest wish). بنده باشم uses باشم (1st-person present-subjunctive of بودن) as an optative — 'may I be / let me be' — so the clause means 'let me be a mere servant'. به درگاه تو places that servant 'at your court/presence' (درگاه = court/presence). Word order is subjectless optative verb phrase + locative complement.
segments.s573-g76: نجویم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive with negative prefix ن- (here functioning as 'I will not seek / I shall not ask'); it continues the optative/hypothetical mood begun previously. جز means 'except' and introduces the sole thing sought. آرایش گاه تو is a genitival/nominal phrase: آرایش 'adornment' + گاه (here functioning like 'place'/'occasion') + تو 'your', so 'except the adornment/place of you' — idiomatically 'I desire nothing but the beauty/favor of your presence.'
segments.s573-g77: بدو is a prepositional/old-fashioned form meaning 'to him/unto him' (introducing the indirect object/recipient of the speech). شاه is the subject 'the king'. گفت here is the main verb 'said' (the word-list gives noun senses for گفت, so no listed sense matches the verbal use). ای is the vocative 'O', followed by two epithets بد ('bad/ill') and بی‌هنر ('without skill / unskilled') addressing the addressee.
segments.s573-g78: چرا کردی is a direct question: چرا 'why' + کردی (2nd-person singular past of کردن) 'did you do'. این بوم is 'this land / this country'. زیر و زبر is an idiomatic paired phrase meaning 'upside down' or 'top-and-bottom' (زیر = under/bottom, زبر = top/above) describing the disturbance; word order places the object after the verb as usual in Persian.
segments.s573-g79: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever/whomever'. بردی is the 2nd-person singular past of بردن 'you took/carried/led (away)'. ز ایران marks origin 'from Iran'. اسیر is the nominal complement 'a captive' modifying 'هرک' — together: 'whoever you have taken captive from Iran.'
segments.s573-g80: همه 'all/everyone' is the direct object to be returned. باز here functions as an adverb 'back/again'. خواهم is 1st-person singular present/future auxiliary 'I will' (from خواستن used to form a future or volitional). ز تو indicates 'from you' (i.e., back from your custody). ناگزیر is a predicative/adverbial meaning 'inevitable/inescapable' or 'compelled' — the line states the speaker's resolve to make the return unavoidable. Note that the full verbal idea 'I will bring/return (them)' is elliptically expressed (خواهم + object + implied main verb), a common poetic compression.
segments.s573-g57: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. بندم here is بند + ـم (possessive) = 'my band/strap/fastening' (a nominal phrase); از introduces the material 'from/out of', چرم خر = 'donkey leather' (چرم 'leather' + خر 'donkey'), and ساختی is 2nd-person past 'you made'. The clause literal order is Why + my-strap + from + material + you-made.
segments.s573-g58: بزرگی is a noun 'greatness/grandeur'; به + خاک اندر is a locative/into-phrase 'into the earth/onto the dust' (به here = 'into/towards'), and انداختی is 2nd-person past 'you threw/cast'. Together: 'you cast greatness into the earth.'
segments.s573-g59: چو here functions as 'as/like' (introducing a likeness); بازارگانان is the plural 'merchants'; به بزم means 'to the feast/assembly' (به = 'into/towards' + بزم 'banquet'); آمدم is 1st-person past 'I came'. The clause reads 'As/like merchants I came to the feast.'
segments.s573-g60: نه negates the following clause ('not'); با introduces accompaniment 'with', کوس 'war-drum' and لشکر 'army/host' are joined by و 'and', به رزم = 'to battle' (به = 'into/towards'), and آمدم is 'I came'. The word order puts the negator before 'با' so: 'I did not come with drum and army to battle.'
segments.s573-g61: تو is the 2nd-person 'you' and مهمان is the predicative noun 'guest' (simple verbless clause 'you are a guest'). به چرم خر اندر is a prepositional/expressive phrase 'in/with donkey leather' (به + چرم + خر + اندر 'in'), and کنی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of کردن 'you do/make' serving as the main verb here ('you are/clothe/put ...'). Overall: 'you are a guest in donkey-leather' (i.e. dressed/treated as such).
segments.s573-g62: به ایران گرایی is a verbal-nominal phrase where به + ایران gives direction 'toward Iran' and گرایی expresses 'turning/leaning toward' (an action/ inclination); و links to لشکر کنی, where لشکر 'army' is the object and کنی (2nd-person present) 'you muster/raise' is the verb. Together: 'and you would (then) turn toward Iran and raise an army.'
segments.s573-g63: ببینی is a present/subjunctive/imperative form of دیدن with the prefix ب- often used in classical Persian to mean 'see/behold' or 'may you see'; کنون means 'now'; جنگ مردان مرد literally 'the battle of men of men' — a superlative/epithet meaning 'the battle of the bravest/most valiant men.' Word order is verb (ببینی) + temporal (کنون) + noun phrase.
segments.s573-g64: کزان is a compound-like form 'from which/whence'; پس here means 'after/thereafter'; نجویی is based on جویی 'search/pursuit' with a negative prefix ن- (meaning 'you will not seek' or 'you will not attempt'); به ایران = 'against/to Iran' and نبرد is the noun 'war/battle' (object). The clause means 'from that time on you will not seek war with Iran.'
segments.s573-g97: This clause contains two coordinated verbs in the second-person (subjunctive/imperative) form connected by و (and). بکاری is the present/subjunctive/optative form meaning “you plant / you should plant,” and دیوارها is the plural object “the walls.” برکنی is the parallel verb (second-person form) meaning “you tear down / you remove.” The sequence sets up actions the addressee is urged to perform.
segments.s573-g98: ز دلها literally “from the hearts” (ز = from; دلها = hearts) marks the domain affected by the previous verbs. مگر introduces a concessive/expectational clause here (rendered as “perhaps / lest / so that one might”), and خشم کمتر کنی is a subordinate clause with کنی (second-person subjunctive/optative of کردن) meaning “you make / cause” and خشم کمتر = “anger less / diminished.” Together it expresses the hoped-for result of the actions.
segments.s573-g99: کنون is an adverb meaning “now”; من is the emphatic subject “I.” به بندی is a noun phrase literally “into/with a binding/band” (به here marks the instrument/means), and ببندم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/optative of بستن meaning “I bind / I will bind.” ترا is the object clitic (‘you’) attached after the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s573-g100: ز چرم خران = “from / of donkey leather” (ز = from/of; چرم = leather; خران = donkeys, here contemptuous). کی is the interrogative “who?” used rhetorically (“who would…?”). پسندم is the verb ‘I like / I approve / I choose’ in which the final -م marks first person; ترا is the second-person object clitic (‘you’), and را is the object marker used for emphasis/definiteness — together پسندم ترا را = “who would choose/accept you.”
segments.s573-g101: گرین is an adverbial/participial descriptor meaning “tearful / weeping” (setting the emotional tone). هرچ گفتم = “whatever I said” or “however much I said” (هرچ = whatever / however much; گفتم = I said). نیاری here functions as a negative/result clause referring to the addressee’s non-arrival or non-response (classical negative + present form meaning ‘you do not/would not come’), and به جای = “to/in the place” (به = to/in; جای = place). The clause expresses lament that despite the speaker’s pleading the addressee did not appear.
segments.s573-g102: بدرند is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive form of دریدن (to tear) meaning “they tear.” چرمت = چرمت (charm + possessive -ت) = “your leather/skin.” ز سر تا به پای is a standard idiom “from head to foot” or “from head as far as the feet” (ز = from; سر = head; تا = as far as/up to; به پای = to the foot). The clause describes violent stripping from head to toe.
segments.s573-g103: دو گوشش = “his two ears” (دو = two; گوش + ش = his ear(s)). به خنجر بدو combines the instrument and direction: به خنجر = “with a dagger” (instrument) and بدو = “to him / into them” (bedo = to him/unto it). شاخ کرد is a causative phrase with کرد ‘made’ and شاخ “horn” — literally “made (them) horns,” i.e. rendered into horn-like stubs by violent action. The clause describes mutilation performed with a dagger.
segments.s573-g104: به یک جای = “in one place / at a single spot” (به = in/at; یک جای = one place/one spot). بینیش is “his nose” (بینی + -ش), and سوراخ کرد is the verb phrase “made a hole / pierced (it).” The segment states that his nose was pierced in a single location.
segments.s573-g17: This is a compact SOV-style clause with the demonstrative first. همان is a demonstrative ('that/the same') pointing to a previously mentioned crown; تاج ایران is the direct object 'crown of Iran'. بدو is the dative 'to him' and در + سپرد together indicate handing 'into his care' (در here has the sense of 'into'). سپرد is the simple past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' (third-person singular). Note the subject ('he' / implied agent) is omitted but supplied from context.
segments.s573-g18: ز گیتی = 'from the world' (ز is the poetic ablative). به جز is the fixed expression 'except' (به + جز). نام زشتی is the object 'a shameful/ignoble name' where نام means 'reputation/name' and زشتی is moral ugliness. نبرد here is an archaic epic form: it functions as the past (3rd sg.) of بردن ('he took/carried away') in Ferdowsi's language (not the noun 'battle'); this is an older poetic usage rather than modern negative formation.
segments.s573-g19: گسسته شد is a predicative participle + auxiliary: گسسته (scattered/broken) + شد (became/was) yields a passive/inchoative meaning 'were scattered' or 'were broken up.' The subject آن لشکر و بارگاه follows (predicate-before-subject ordering is common in poetry). و simply links 'army' and 'palace.'
segments.s573-g20: به نیروی یزدان is an instrumental phrase 'by the power/strength of God' (به here has the instrumental/adverbial sense 'by'). که introduces a relative clause 'who/which' and بنمود is the simple past of نمودن 'to show' (he showed); راه is the direct object 'the way/path.' Note the relative clause explains the means: by divine power, who showed the way.
segments.s573-g21: هرانکس که باشد ز رومی به شهر is a general relative clause: هرانکس 'whoever' + که introduces the relative clause. باشد is the classical present/subjunctive form used here to mean 'is' / 'happens to be' (a generalizing present). ز رومی = 'of/from the Romans' (ablative/attributive: 'Roman'), and به شهر sets the place 'in the city/town.'
segments.s573-g22: ز شمشیر uses ز (here instrumental/ablative) with شمشیر 'by the sword' (i.e., 'with the sword'). باید که introduces necessity 'it is necessary / they must' and governs a subjunctive clause: یابند is the present-subjunctive/optative form 'they should find / they be found' (classical usage: subjunctive after باید که). بهر at the end is adverbial 'for / for the purpose of' (here marking purpose/interest). The sense is idiomatic: those found in the town among the Romans must be met/handled by the sword.
segments.s573-g23: این یک دستور جمعی: 'همه داد جویید و فرمان کنید' — a pair of imperatives/optatives. داد جویید is a compound noun+verb meaning 'seek justice' (جویید is the present/subjunctive/imperative form 'seek'). فرمان کنید is an imperative/optative 'give orders' or 'issue commands' (کنید is the polite/plural imperative form). The two verbal phrases are coordinated by و.
segments.s573-g24: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'in goodness / in goodwill' (به here functioning adverbially 'by/with' + خوبی 'goodness'). ز سر باز is an idiomatic classical expression meaning 'anew / from the start' (ز سر = 'from the head/from the beginning', باز = 'again'), so the clause means 'renew from the start.' پیمان کنید is the imperative 'make a covenant / conclude a treaty' (کنید as the plural/polite imperative). Together: 'in goodwill, remake the treaty from the beginning.'
segments.s573-g81: This fragment lists the thing being warned about. دگر here means "other/else/any more" (i.e. "whatever else"). خواسته is a nominal/participial element meaning "that which is wished/desired" and combines with هرچ ("whatever") to yield "whatever (else)"; the verb بردی (archaic second‑person past/present form of بردن) is the action "you have taken/sent" (object understood as the things mentioned). به + روم gives the destination "to Rome." Word order is topical: the phrase names the items first, then the verb and destination.
segments.s573-g82: مبادا introduces a negative jussive warning: "lest/May it not be that..." followed by que/که introducing the subordinate clause. بینی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form "you see/may you see" used after مبادا to express the feared outcome. تو is the explicit subject "you." آن بوم is the noun phrase "that land/home" and شوم here is the adjective/ predicate "ill‑omened/unlucky" (not a literal 1st‑person verb), so the clause means "lest you see that land (to be) ill‑omened."
segments.s573-g83: همه یکسر is an adverbial/quantifying phrase: همه = "all/everyone" and یکسر = "entirely/at once/altogether." از خانه gives the source/location "from home." بازآوری is the verb (poetic/older form of بازآوردن) addressed to the addressee: here it functions as a 2nd‑person present/imperative meaning "bring back/recall/restore." The sequence therefore commands: "bring all (people) back from the house at once."
segments.s573-g84: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase (به + این) meaning "to this." It links to لشکر "army/host," the indirect object or destination. سرفراز is an adjective modifying لشکر meaning "victorious/proud/honorable." آوری is the verb (same root as بازآوری but here repeated) in the 2nd‑person present/imperative sense "bring (to)" — so the whole segment reads "bring (them) to this victorious army."
segments.s573-g85: This is a locative/clausal clause: از ایران "in/from Iran" with هرانجا که "wherever that" introducing the relative clause. ویران is the predicate adjective "ruined/devastated," and شدست is an archaic compound form meaning "has become/has been" (equivalent to modern شده‌است). Together: "Wherever in Iran that it has been ruined..."
segments.s573-g86: کنام here means "the bosom/haunt/seat" (metaphorically: dwelling or refuge) and takes پلنگان و شیران as its possessors/occupants: "of leopards and lions." شدست repeats the archaic copula "has become/has turned into," so the clause states that the ruined places "have become the haunts of leopards and lions." Note the implicit subject continuity from the previous clause; شدست refers back to the places just described.
segments.s573-g87: سراسر is an adverb meaning "throughout/everywhere." برآری is a verbal form (from برآوردن/برآورد) here meaning roughly "you will bring about/you will expend/you will obtain" in the 2nd‑person; in context it conveys spending or using money widely. به دینار خویش links the means/thing used: به + دینار = "with/by your dinar(s)," and خویش + ش is the possessive "your/one's own." The phrase thus says that everywhere you use/pay your dinars.
segments.s573-g88: بیابی is the 2nd‑person present/future or subjunctive form of یافتن "to find" meaning "you will find" (poetic/future sense). مکافات here means "retribution/punishment" (negative consequence rather than reward). کردار خویش = "your deeds/your actions" (خویش + ش again marking possessive). Together: "you will find the retribution of your deeds."
segments.s573-g89: This clause begins with the conditional/contrast particle دگر + an indefinite pronoun هرک 'whoever/anyone', followed by the object 'کشتی ز ایرانیان' (ship(s) from the Iranians). Word order is topic/condition (دگر هرک) → verb/nominal object; here 'کشتی' is the thing taken and 'ز ایرانیان' is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the Iranians'.
segments.s573-g90: بجویی is an exhortative/subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'seek' or 'demand' (addressing the addressee). It takes the following prepositional phrase 'ز روم' ('from/out of Rome') and the genitive 'از نژاد کیان' ('of the stock/lineage of the Kians'), which specifies the people in Rome to be held responsible. In classical Persian the order 'بجویی ز روم از نژاد کیان' is natural: verb → place source → further specification.
segments.s573-g91: This short clause uses 'به یک تن' in the sense 'for each person / per person' (به + numeral + تن = 'per one person'), then 'از روم' marks the source/location, and 'تاوان دهی' is a present/future hortative 'you shall give/pay compensation'. Word order: adverbial ('به یک تن') → source ('از روم') → object ('تاوان') → verb ('دهی').
segments.s573-g92: روان را is the definite object (را) with روان ('soul' or poetically 'person'); به پیمان گروگان دهی is a verbal construction meaning 'give (them) as hostages under treaty/covenant.' The preposition به introduces the instrumental/benefactive phrase 'به پیمان' ('under the covenant'), and گروگان is the noun 'hostage' that the verb دهی ('give') governs.
segments.s573-g93: نخواهم is the first-person negative of 'خواستن' used here to mean 'I wish / I want not' (i.e., 'I want none'), followed by the restrictive phrase 'به جز' meaning 'except'. 'مرد قیصرنژاد' is the exception: 'a man of Caesar-born / imperial stock.' The structure is: main verb (negative wish) → exception marker → noun phrase.
segments.s573-g94: This relative clause begins with که 'who/that' and uses باشند (3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative in classical Persian) to mean 'who shall be / who will be / who are (to be)'. با ما 'with us' is the accompanying prepositional phrase. بدین بوم is a fused locative 'با/به + این' = 'in/at this land' (here tokenized as ب + دین in the line), and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy'. Overall: 'who will be with us in this land, happy.'
segments.s573-g95: دگر هرچ 'whatever else/anything more' is a generalizing phrase; ز ایران بریدی is a perfect/past second‑person clause 'you cut/tore from Iran' (بریدی = 'you cut/you took away'). The final noun درخت ('tree') is placed after the verb as a direct object or topic: 'whatever else you cut from Iran — a tree.' The order (indefinite → source → past verb → object) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s573-g96: This short, elliptical line is compressed and poetical. نبرد here is ambiguous in the manuscript tradition: it can read as the noun 'battle/strife' or (more likely in context) as the negated compound 'ن + برد' meaning 'did not take/carry' — classical texts often allow such ambiguity. The sequence 'درخت گشن نیک‌بخت' strings together nouns/adjectives: 'tree / hungry / fortunate', producing a terse, image-like phrase. The exact syntactic relations are compressed by poetic economy, so the English rendering above reflects that condensation rather than an unambiguous prose syntax.
segments.s573-g9: که is the relative 'who/that' introducing a clause about the person. اوراست is an older fused form of او + راست meaning 'he/it is' (here identifying the subject). بر + نیکویی + دست‌رس is a prepositional phrase literally 'on goodness (is) reach' — Persian places the possessed/characteristic noun after بر; together 'اوراست بر نیکویی دست‌رس' = 'he is (one) whose reach/hand comes upon goodness' (i.e. 'he can reach goodness').
segments.s573-g10: به نیرو is a prepositional phrase 'for/with respect to strength'. نیازش is the object 'his need' (ش enclitic = 'his'). نیاید is the classical present/negative form of آمدن 'to come' meaning 'does not come/arrive'. به کس ('to/for anyone') completes the idea: 'his need for strength does not come to anyone' — i.e. no one supplies (or is owed) his need for strength.
segments.s573-g11: همو is emphatic 'he himself / that same one' placed before the title. آفریننده is a noun 'Creator' and روزگار ('the ages'/'times') is the object/genitive complement; the noun phrase reads 'he himself, the Creator of the ages.' The order (emphatic pronoun + title + domain) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s573-g12: به نیکی is an ablative/agentive phrase here 'by/through goodness' (به used like English 'by'). همو repeats the emphatic pronoun 'he himself'. باشد is the classical optative/subjunctive 3rd-person form of بودن meaning 'may be / is' used here as present 'is'. آموزگار is the predicative noun 'teacher' — together 'in goodness he himself is (the) teacher.'
segments.s573-g13: چو is the comparative/simile 'as/like'. قیصر is the proper name 'Caesar' (the exemplar). که introduces a relative clause 'who'. فرمان یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the command/decree of God' (فرمان governed by یزدان). بهشت stands as the theme/object ('paradise') that is linked to what God commanded; the actual verbal action (brought/placed) appears in the next segment, so this segment sets up 'Caesar who, by God's command, (regarding) paradise...'.
segments.s573-g14: به ایران is 'into/to Iran' (destination). به جز is the idiom 'except' (به + جز). تخم زشتی is 'the seed of ugliness' where تخم = 'seed' and زشتی = 'ugliness/evil' (here moral ugliness). نکشت is the verb in the past negative or classical form meaning 'he planted / he did not plant' in context functioning as the main verb ('he sowed there nothing but...'); the exact classical tense/form is archaic, but the clause means 'he planted there nothing except the seed of ugliness.'
segments.s573-g15: به زاری is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with lamentation / by lamenting'. همی is the archaic/progressive particle 'still / continuously'. بند here means 'stanza' (a unit of verse) rather than a physical fetter. ساید is an older adjectival/verb form meaning 'prevails / is current' and کنون means 'now' — together 'now a stanza of lamentation still prevails.'
segments.s573-g16: چو here means 'when/as' introducing a comparative clause. جان را uses را as the object marker 'the soul'. نبودش is the classical negative past/present idiom 'there was not / is not' (here 'lacks'). خرد رهنمون is a noun + noun 'wisdom (خرد) (as) guide (رهنمون)' — the phrase means 'a guiding wisdom.' So the clause reads 'as when the soul lacks a guiding wisdom.'
segments.s573-g41: بشد is a past/past‑resultive verbal form here meaning 'it came to pass / he acted' (classical epic style often uses ب- + شد). روزبان is a proper name subject; دست is a concrete noun 'hand' with no preposition (object of گرفت/بشد implicitly); قیصرکشان is a compound plural 'the men who carry/escort the Caesar' (کشان = those who drag/carry), so the sequence reads literally 'Rozbān [beshod] the hand of the Caesar‑bearers' i.e. he seized them.
segments.s573-g42: ز زندان بیاورد is a short clause with ز = 'from' plus زندان 'prison' and بیاورد (past/remote subjunctive/past narrative form here) 'he brought (out)'. چون is the simile/temporal 'as/like' and بیهشان is an archaic construction (بیه + شان) meaning roughly 'without them' or 'bereft (of them)'; overall: 'he brought them from prison, as if without (anything left)'.
segments.s573-g43: جفادیده is a descriptive noun/adjective 'the wronged/afflicted one' used as subject. چون before a verb here functions temporally 'when/as' and introduces the following clause; روی شاپور دید = 'he saw Shāpūr's face' (روی = face, شاپور proper name, دید = past 'saw').
segments.s573-g44: سرشکش is a poetic noun 'a little drop / teardrop' (سرشک = tear) with the possessive/nominal sense suppressed; ز دیده = 'from the eye' (ز = from, دیده = eye), به رخ بر چکید is a light‑verb clause 'fell onto the cheek' where به رخ = 'onto the cheek' and چکید is the past 'it dripped/fell'. Word order puts the source (ز دیده) before the target (به رخ) as is common in poetry.
segments.s573-g45: بمالید is the simple past of مالیدن 'to rub/smear', here transitive and placed before its object (typical epic verb‑first order). رنگین رخش = 'his colored/stained face' (رنگین = colored; رخش = رخ + possessive ش 'his'; note رخش here is 'his countenance', not the horse sense), and بر زمین 'on the earth/ground' is the locative.
segments.s573-g46: همی is the archaic continuous particle meaning 'kept on / repeatedly'; کرد is the past of کردن 'to do/say' so همی کرد = 'kept on doing/uttering'. بر تاج و تخت آفرین literally '(uttered) praise upon crown and throne' where آفرین is an acclamation/noun 'praise' or interjection 'bravo'.
segments.s573-g47: زمین را سراسر is an object/topic phrase 'the earth throughout' with را marking it definite/accusative; به مژگان = 'with eyelashes' (poetically meaning 'by his lashes' i.e. by falling tears) and برفت is a past verb 'fell/was strewn' (poetic perfective) meaning the earth was so covered — the whole clause: 'he covered the earth all over with lashes/tears.'
segments.s573-g48: به موی و به روی گشت = 'to/onto his hair and onto his face it turned' where گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' (here 'became smeared'), و joins the two targets. با خاک جفت is a poetic verbless phrase 'paired with dust' (= 'joined with the dust, smeared with dust'); جفت here means 'joined/paired with'.
segments.s573-g65: This is a short clause of direct speech introduction. بدو is the old/poetic prepositional phrase “to him” placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense reporting verb “said” (here used in the simple past). قیصر is the subject after the verb (verb–subject order is common in classical/poetic Persian). که‌ای شهریار is a vocative: که‌ای is an archaic/poetic marker + ای for calling, and شهریار is the addressee “O king.”
segments.s573-g66: ز فرمان یزدان is an ablative/agent phrase “by/from the command of God” (ز + noun is a poetic equivalent of از). که introduces an optative/subjunctive clause here (‘that/may’), and یابد is the subjunctive/optative form ‘may he find/attain’ (the listed lexical sense indicating imperative/subjunctive fits this use). گذار is the object meaning “passage/way.”
segments.s573-g67: ز من is a prepositional phrase “from me” placed up front for emphasis. بخت is the grammatical subject ‘fortune/fate,’ and شاها is the intervening vocative “O king.” خرد دور کرد is a past-tense transitive clause: خرد (wisdom) is the object and دور کرد literally “made (it) distant” = “drove away/removed.” The main verb کرد is past simple causative/performative.
segments.s573-g68: روانم is the possessed noun “my spirit/soul” (the -م suffix). بر here means “to/for” (indicating the beneficiary/target), دیو مزدور is a noun phrase ‘a mercenary/hireling of the demon,’ and کرد again is the past verb “made” (caused to be). Word order places the possessed noun before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s573-g69: This is a conditional clause: مکافات is ‘retribution/punishment’ (the thing given), بدگر ‘the evildoer/bad person’ is the recipient, and کنی is the present/subjunctive “you do/give” (used to form the protasis ‘if you do X’). نیکوی modifies the action — here adverbially ‘well/properly’ or ‘with merit.’ The clause thus reads roughly ‘if you deal punishment to the evildoer rightly…’.
segments.s573-g70: به گیتی درون is a locative expression ‘throughout/in the world’ (به + گیتی + درون emphasizing inside the world). داستانی is predicative/nominative ‘a tale/story’ and شوی is the present/subjunctive of شدن ‘become’ (here indicating the apodosis/result: ‘you will become a story’). The overall sense is consequence/result of the preceding conditional.
segments.s573-g71: که is the subordinating particle introducing the result/aim clause. هرگز is the negative adverb ‘never,’ and نگردد is a negative subjunctive/passive-ish form of گشت/گردیدن ‘to become/turn’ — here ‘may not become/ be changed.’ کهن modifies نام: کهن نام تو = ‘your ancient/old name’ (i.e. your long-standing reputation).
segments.s573-g72: برآید is an optative/subjunctive form of برآمدن ‘to come about/ be realized’ (wish/result: ‘may … come to pass’). به مردی uses مردی in the abstract sense ‘manliness/manly deed’ (not just a single man), indicating the medium/way, and همه کام تو is the object ‘all your desires/wishes.’ Together the clause expresses a wish that all the addressee’s desires be fulfilled through manly deeds.
segments.s573-g49: 'بدو گفت' is an indirect-object + verb sequence meaning 'said to him' (literally 'to him said'); Persian often places the indirect object before the verb. 'شاه‌ای' is a vocative with -ای ('O king' or 'O ruler') used to address someone directly. 'سراسر بدی' is a predicate/adjectival phrase 'entirely evil' used as the form of address (literally 'all-over evil'), i.e. 'O wholly evil one.'
segments.s573-g50: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (‘who/for’). ترسایی functions as a substantive/adjectival idea 'fearfulness' or 'cowardice' describing the addressee. و links the two qualities. دشمن ایزدی = 'an enemy of the divine' with ایزدی as an adjectival/genitive attribute 'divine' (of God). The clause characterizes the person just addressed.
segments.s573-g51: پسر is a vocative 'son' used to address the hearer. گویی here (poetic) means 'you say' / 'you claim' or 'it is as if you say' (a present-reported sense). آنرا is the accusative pronoun 'it' (that). کش انباز is an opaque compound in this line; syntactically کش is the verbal root 'to pull/seize' and انباز is an old/uncertain word modifying it — together meaning something like 'to seize/hold by force' (hence the sense 'cannot be seized'). نیست negates the clause 'is not / does not exist.'
segments.s573-g52: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (or 'in' depending on idiom). گیتیش = گیتِ + -ش, 'his/its world' (possessive -ش). فرجام و آغاز نیست = 'there is no end and no beginning' — فرجام 'end/outcome', آغاز 'beginning', with نیست as existential negation. Word order places the negated predicate after the possessional phrase.
segments.s573-g53: ندانی is the negative present (or rhetorical negative) meaning 'you do not know' (second-person). تو is emphatic 'you'. گفتن سخن (inverted order from the ordinary سخن گفتن) = 'to say speech' i.e. 'to speak' — گفتن is the infinitive 'to say' and سخن 'words/speech'. جز دروغ = 'except lies' modifies سخن: 'you know how to say nothing except lies.'
segments.s573-g54: This is a nominal-predicative clause: 'دروغ آتشی بد بود بی‌فروغ' = 'a lie was a bad, fiery thing without brightness.' دروغ is the subject 'a lie'; آتشی ('fiery') and بد ('bad') are attributive adjectives; بود is the past copula 'was'; بی‌فروغ is a postpositive adjective 'without radiance' describing the lie.
segments.s573-g55: اگر begins a conditional ('if'). قیصری refers to 'the Caesar' or emperor/imperial figure (used here as a proper noun or epithet). شرم و رایت = 'shame (honor/modesty) and the standard/banner' (two attributes or possessions). کجا ... ست is the interrogative 'where is ...?' with کجا 'where' and ست the copula 'is' (classical/poetic form). The clause rhetorically asks, 'If the emperor has shame and a banner, where are they?'
segments.s573-g56: به خوبی دل is a genitive/qualifying phrase 'to/by the goodness of the heart' (more idiomatically 'the good guidance of the heart'). رهنمایت is the noun 'guide/guidance' with a pronominal/poetic ending (-ت) making it 'your/its guide' or simply part of the compound 'رهنمایی' meaning 'guidance'; here it denotes 'good counsel' or 'guide.' کجاست (کجا + ست) again asks 'where is it?' — so the whole clause asks, 'And where is good heart-guidance?'

sentences.s573-s1: When night drew the skirt over the day, the sun's banner came forth from on high.
sentences.s573-s2: Shapur commanded that a scribe be appointed; he asked for a pen and anqās (?), and musk and silk.
sentences.s573-s3: They wrote letters to every chief, to every king and to every country (realm).
sentences.s573-s4: He made the heading 'Afarin, O guests' (or 'Praise, O guests'); from us (a) servant toward the Creator of the world.
sentences.s573-s53: He fitted a bridle to his nose, as Shapur did — from that donkey-hide leather he made a memento.
sentences.s573-s54: He set two heavy fetters on his feet; that same Rozban then took him back to his place.
sentences.s573-s13: Monstrous creatures rose up from every side of the skirts, with the king's letter, the bright‑souled.
sentences.s573-s14: From the army‑camp he came toward Ctesiphon and, harmless, sat down with a guide.
sentences.s573-s15: When he set the crown of his ancestors upon his head, he remembered the Creator’s gift of goodness.
sentences.s573-s16: He ordered that the scribe be put into prison; he wrote the captive’s name at/in Anqās.
sentences.s573-s17: The number came to one thousand one hundred and ten — the great men of Rome, those of ill repute.
sentences.s573-s18: All his own and Caesar's kin were in Rome's inner quarters, each a distinguished elder.
sentences.s573-s19: The sovereign cut off their hands and feet — whoever was an evil guide to evil.
sentences.s573-s20: He ordered that they bring the Caesar of Rome, the ruler of that land.
sentences.s573-s37: If I obtain it from you — by my life, take heed — then in my eyes treasure and dinars will be worthless.
sentences.s573-s38: Let me be but a humble servant at your court; I will seek nothing except the adornment of your place.
sentences.s573-s39: To him the king said, 'O ill and unskilled one, why did you make this land upside down?'
sentences.s573-s40: Now, whoever you have taken captive from Iran — I will certainly (bring) them all back to you; it is unavoidable.
sentences.s573-s29: Why did you make my strap from donkey leather, and cast greatness into the dust?
sentences.s573-s30: As I came to the feast like merchants, I did not come to battle with drum and host.
sentences.s573-s31: You are a guest clad in donkey leather, and you would turn toward Iran and raise an army.
sentences.s573-s32: Now behold the battle of the bravest of men; from that forthyou will no longer seek to wage war on Iran.
sentences.s573-s49: Plant (goodwill) and tear down the walls in people's hearts — perhaps you will make their anger diminish.
sentences.s573-s50: Now I will bind you in a bond; who would take a strap of donkey-hide for you?
sentences.s573-s51: Though I said whatever (I could) with tears, you did not come in that place; they tear your hide from head to foot.
sentences.s573-s52: They drove a dagger into both his ears, making them like horns; they pierced his nose in one place.
sentences.s573-s9: He handed that crown of Iran to him; from the world he carried away nothing except a shameful name.
sentences.s573-s10: That army and the palace were broken apart, by the power of God, who showed the way.
sentences.s573-s11: Whoever of the Romans is in the town — by the sword they must be dealt with.
sentences.s573-s12: All should seek justice and issue commands; in goodwill, renew (make) the treaty afresh.
sentences.s573-s41: And whatever else you have sent to Rome, beware lest you find that land unlucky for you.
sentences.s573-s42: Bring everyone back home at once, and bring them to this victorious army.
sentences.s573-s43: Wherever in Iran it has been laid waste, it has become the haunt of leopards and lions.
sentences.s573-s44: Throughout (the land) you will spend your dinars; you will find the recompense (retribution) for your deeds.
sentences.s573-s45: If anyone else seizes ships from the Iranians, seek him in Rome — of the royal stock of the Kians.
sentences.s573-s46: For each person you shall give ten from Rome as compensation; you shall give the souls as hostages by covenant.
sentences.s573-s47: I want none except a man of Caesar’s stock who will be with us in this land, happy.
sentences.s573-s48: Whatever else you cut from Iran — (as for) the tree: the tree did not wage battle; the hungry one (was) fortunate.
sentences.s573-s5: who has access to goodness; his need for strength does not come to anyone.
sentences.s573-s6: He himself is the Creator of the ages; in goodness he himself is the teacher.
sentences.s573-s7: Like Caesar, who by God's command (was to bring) paradise to Iran, he planted there nothing but the seed of ugliness.
sentences.s573-s8: Now a stanza of lamentation still prevails, as when the soul has no guiding wisdom.
sentences.s573-s21: Rozbān seized the hands of the men who bore the Caesar and brought them out of the prison, as though bereft.
sentences.s573-s22: When the wronged man saw Shāpūr’s face, a teardrop fell from his eye upon his cheek.
sentences.s573-s23: He smeared his stained face on the ground and kept crying out praise for crown and throne.
sentences.s573-s24: He covered the earth entirely with (his) lashes; his hair and his face became joined with the dust.
sentences.s573-s33: Caesar said to him, “O king, by God’s command may he find a way.”
sentences.s573-s34: Fortune, O king, has driven wisdom from me, and has made my spirit a hireling of a demon.
sentences.s573-s35: If you duly punish the evildoer, you will become a tale throughout the world.
sentences.s573-s36: so that your ancient name may never be altered; may all your wishes be fulfilled by manliness.
sentences.s573-s25: The king said to him, "O wholly evil one — you are fearful and an enemy of the divine."
sentences.s573-s26: You say, my son, that it cannot be seized; in his world there is neither end nor beginning.
sentences.s573-s27: You do not know, you, how to speak anything except lies; a lie was a bad, fiery thing without radiance.
sentences.s573-s28: If the Caesar has shame and a standard, where are they? And where is the good guidance of the heart?
